sentences.text: [BEEPING]
Gutreich.
My name is Stanley Goodrich, previously [?
Samuel ?] Gutreich.
I was born in Nowy Targ, Poland, which is a small town in southern Poland.
And I'm a son of-- my father's name was Chanine Gutreich.
My mother's name was Mala Gutreich.
I had a brother and a sister.
And an [? aunt. ?] Pardon me?
My, my sister's name was Edita.
My brother's name was Heinek.
My last name is Goodrich, G-O-O-D-R-I-C-H.
At birth was Gutreich, G-U-T-R-E-I-C-H.
July the 13th, 1924.
Yes.
It's, it's a small town.
It's about-- population about 12,000 people.
Between-- with 12,000 people, about 2,000 people-- [INAUDIBLE] 2,000 persons were Jewish, about 300 families.
Like I said, it was a small, little town, and right, right next to-- next to the Czechoslovakian border.
We had about seven kilometers to the Czechoslovakian border.
Oh yeah.
It was Poland.
Sure.
Well, there were a few synagogues.
And there were some, for the young people, were Zionist organizations, like, you know, Hashomer Hatzair and, and Hashomer Hadati.
It's some [INAUDIBLE], I guess.
You know, it was quite a few for young people.
We joined the Jewish Zionist organization.
About 12,000.
I mean, the Polish population?
I would think about 10,000, 10,000, and 2,000 Jews.
It was, you know, fairly good.
We didn't [INAUDIBLE].
But anti-Semitism was quite high in Poland, as you know.
But we didn't-- we didn't felt it so bad in this small town.
Was not as bad as maybe we read in the newspapers from the big cities.
We got along pretty well with our neighbors, which were Polish.
And we, we lived in our own house.
It was surrounded by Polish people, mostly farmers.
It was a-- was a little town, no industries, just strictly, you know, farming, farming, all, all around farms, you know, little family farms.
Well, my, my grandfather, I guess, he-- which, which he was born in Nowy Targ going way, way back.
And he started a, a store, which it was a store with, you know, like, dry goods store.
And then my father married my mother, which in 1920s.
And they took over that store.
And also, we had, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, a little grocery store, so in other words, we combined textile and grocery store.
And this one we made a living from.
No, no, the farms-- we didn't have any products coming from the farms.

Most of the farmers came to town to buy-- to buy just food for themselves, because it was-- it was a poor country, I mean that particularly in the neighborhood, which, like I told you, was in the mountains, close to the Czechoslovakian border.
And the farmers usually would grow potatoes.
And what else did they grow?
Cabbage and hay.
We didn't grow any wheat or some food, better food.
So they used to come to town and buy textile to-- for clothing, for their-- for their own clothing.
And also, they used to buy food, like, you know, flour and sugar and salt, whatever they needed.
And, and that's about it.
So we supplied them with all these needs.
Yes.
My grandfather was very religious, you know.
He used to have a beard-- used to have a beard.
My father was religious, also religious, but he was moderate religious.
The kids, we were brought out in a religious home.
Used to go to, to cheder when I was a kid, and then to Hebrew school.
And I belonged to a Zionist organization.
And I started, you know, that as a teenager, about 11, 12 years old.
Yes.
Polish.
Yeah, but we-- my grandmother and grandfather used to speak Polish, but-- I mean Jewish.
But we spoke quite a bit Polish in, in our-- in our town.
Was very peculiar in a town like, like Jews speaking Polish.
But there was religious Jews speaking Polish.
Was great.
You know, I had a great childhood.
I, I enjoyed my life to be, you know, with the family and the friends.
I had a lot of friends.
And it was a strict family life.
You know, we didn't go out and having parties, like the kids-- teenagers doing here.
We had to go to school and then to cheder, but we had time to play and enjoy life.
Right.
Oh, yeah.
Of course.
We had the radio in the house.
And I remember we-- my father used to listen, the kids, too, also, listen to the speeches of Hitler, Mussolini and Hitler.
And we knew that something is brewing.
But as a kid, I didn't-- I didn't expect that the war would break out.
We knew what's going on-- we knew what's going on with the Jews from Germany, which they were deported to Poland.
And we knew I had a [INAUDIBLE] living in Vienna.
He was a brother of my mother's.
And in 1938, when the-- when the Germans marched into Vienna, to Austria, they, they took him to Dachau, was a concentration camp, Dachau.
And we had-- we had letters from, from my-- from my aunt or his wife, from his wife, to do something because he was still a Polish citizen.
He didn't acquire the, the Austrian citizenship, as you know.
Nobody could if they came from Poland.
And to help him to get a, a, a visa to come back to Poland, to help him out to get-- to get out of the concentration camp.
So we knew what's going on in Germany.
And he was in the-- in, in Dachau.
And--
We know that because he was Jewish.
And that he was Jewish and they took the Jews to, to camps or concentration camps.
And we couldn't do-- I remember my mother and my-- and my father, my, my aunts, they went and they tried to get some, some papers for my uncle to come back to Poland.
They couldn't-- they couldn't get it.
The Polish government wouldn't, wouldn't allow to-- any Jews to come back to Poland.
So luckily, they all found out-- we, we, we knew the-- before the war started, I think, I remember we got a postcard from him.
Yes.
It was before the war.
They somehow luckily got him out of, of Dachau to get to Cuba.
They bought some visas to go to Cuba.
And they went out on the-- on that ship, as you know, on the St.
Louis.
They went to Cuba.
We, we remember-- I remember that we used to get cards from him.
From the boat-- from the-- from the ship I get.
They were boarding the ship, still going to Cuba.
And then the war broke out.
We never knew what happened with him.
So you don't know if he was one of the people that perished after the ship came back?
No, he survived.
Yes, he survived.
And because they came to-- like I said, they came to, to Havana, to Cuba.
The Cuban government wouldn't let him in.
And the St.
Louis went back to Europe.
And luckily, him and his wife and one daughter, who was a little baby, they disembarked in England.
And he survived the war in England.
Name is Mechal Blumenstock.
No.
He passed away in 1972.
His daughter, his wife-- his daughter's still alive.
She's quite active in the-- what is it-- United States Memorial Museum, Holocaust Museum.
She's a vice, vice chairman.
Well, I, I never had any special conversation with him, how it was on the boat.
But he was telling me that it was tragic to, you know, come so far and then to go back.
And I, I never asked him about the experiences.
But I know only one thing, that he came back to England.
And when the war started, the Polish government want to take him in as-- you know, they, they called him in to, to, to serve in the Polish army, which he said, the heck with you.
You didn't want to take me into Poland when I was-- in 1938.
I begged you to come back to Poland.
And he joined the English army during the war.
And he served in the English army.
He did, yeah.
No, I don't.
No.
OK.
Oh, yeah.
I was already 15 years old, as you know.
I guess that they must have been afraid, because, you know, my father in 1939, when the war started, he run away from, from the hometown.
He was so afraid of the Germans.
And since 1945, since September 1, 1945, I've never seen my father again.
You know, we were the first-- like I just tell you before, we were on the border, the Czechoslovakian border.
And the Germans marched in right away, the 1st of September, that they attacked-- when they attacked Poland.
And lots of people-- I guess they planned it like that, or they, they, they knew about it.
They-- just by foot, they, they, they run on the first day when the Germans attacked.
They just left Nowy-- Poland-- Nowy-- not Poland, Nowy Targ.
And they, they moved to different-- to far away from the front, you know, not to be caught by the Germans.
They were-- they were going east.
They were going east.
And he ended, ended up in Lemberg, in Lvov.
And we used to get, you know, regards from him, from Lemberg.
And then my-- we had an uncle living in, in Wilno, which is in Lithuania.
It was Poland then.
But after the Russians attack Poland in September, they took over the, you know, the eastern part of Poland.
And they, they granted independence to Lithuania.
Wilno was in Lithuania then.
And we had an uncle living in Wilno.
And my uncle-- my father smuggled himself from Lemberg to Wilno to be with his-- with his brother.
And he thought he's going to survive the war in Wilno.
We used to get letters from him from Wilno.
And 1941, when the Germans came in, they, they killed all the Jews in, in-- not all of it, but most of the Jews they killed in Wilno.
And that's, that's-- I never have seen my father since September the 1st, 1939.
That was the last time I see him, my father.
Yes.
And I want you to know, he was not the only one.
Was quite a lot of Jews, people, you know, Jewish people in the age-- his age, I guess, which they could-- they could still run away, they ran away.
It was, was without explanation to my mother, I guess, you know, why he left.
But we have to-- we lived with it.
We didn't know.
Because a lot of people came back from, from, from, from Lemberg and from Russia, which they, they got killed anyway.
But my mother was left with, with three kids, myself and a brother and a sister.
And, and, and, and I guess, you know, my father, he was afraid to come back.
They thought maybe the, the war will-- the war will, will end.
And, and will, will, you know, they, they were telling people that the, the Germans killing off the Jewish men, or the taking away the Jewish men.
It was the reason they run away.
Yes.
In a way I felt abandoned.
I wished that my father would be with us.
Would be easier on my mother and myself.
Right, right.
Oh, yeah.
Well, a lot-- a lot of them ran away.
And, and, and some of them survived in Russia, about the same age as my father.
They run away.
Their family never survived their wives, in some cases, never survived.
But I know some people from Nowy Targ which ran away to Russia.
They survived in Russia.
Good friends of mine, their parents, their fathers, I mean, not their parents, their fathers.
So that's how it was.
Well, my mother had a hard time to, to keep everybody, you know, alive and fed and everything.
We had-- like I tell you, we had the store with, with textiles and, and, and grocery [INAUDIBLE] textiles.
And my mother used to sell out, in the beginning, some textiles.
And we had some money.
And we managed.
We had some-- like I tell you, we lived between Polish people.
And, like, it was a-- was a Polish town, because there were out of 12,000 people, only 2,000 Jews.
So we had a lot of neighbors who were Jews.
And we dealt with farmers, which they lived around the villages, so we-- they helped us out in a way with food.
Used to get a little bit more food that, that, that anybody else, on carts, you know.
So my mother had a very hard time.
But we helped-- we lived together with my grandmother, the grandmother and grandfather.
And that's how we managed.
What were their names, your grandmother and your grandfather?
Their names?
Was my-- that's from my mother's side.
His name was Mayer Blumenstock.
And his wife was Mina Blumenstock.
And they, they survived-- I mean, they lived during the war until they got killed by the Germans.
Right.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
There was-- not everybody, but people which they had contact with, with Polacks, you know, they were helped, because you know, it was very hard to survive.
And if you could get anything, it was a big help.
Some wood, you know, wood to burn-- for burning in your stove, or some potatoes, or some, you know, some, some things, you know, that you could survive.
Well, when my father left and the war broke out, when the Germans came in, they took me right away to work.
Not, not me, but I mean, not, not-- the Germans didn't take, take us.
They formed a Judenrat, who were the-- some Jews, you know, quite well, well, well-known Jews from the city, like [INAUDIBLE].
They were not leaders before the war.
The Germans made them leaders.
Well, I don't know.
I don't know why they picked them.
I don't know.
Maybe through some-- you know, you know, through-- they went to, maybe, to the Polish-- what they called the Polish--
[POLISH], what they call it?
The Polish mayor and they asked him who is between the Jews which, which we should appoint to be a Judenrat?
So they must have told them, hey, they are the well-known Jews from a nice family.
And what they did, they asked right away for some, some young people to, to, to supply young people to work.
So what did the Judenrat do?
The Judenrat picked up some young people, the age of between 15 and 20.
They wouldn't, you know, send some people which are, let's say, a little bit older or married or something to, to go to do manual work.
That was the beginning of.
So we used to go to work.
You know, the Germans were occupying us.
We had a lot of German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do they call it?
The police and Gestapo, which they occupied in Nowy Targ.
And also, which they-- what they, they call it?
Yeah, I'm, I'm thinking about it.
I'm thinking about it.
You know, in the interview, it's kind of hard to, to, to, to explain.
They had a border patrol.
They had a border patrol, which they, you know, they, they-- we had a border, like I just mentioned.
There were a lot of patrol-- German [? patrol. ?] And we worked for him, like cleaning cars and cleaning their houses and shoveling snow.
And that was manual work.

And slowly, slowly, they took more people in from the-- the Judenrat called more people in, more people in to different kind of work, to building roads, to building barracks, and so forth, to building-- to building an airport, which was not too much of an airport, but you know, to fix an airport, stuff like that.
Every, every week, every, every month, more people started to be employed by the Germans.
Now they would have to work for the Germans.
No.
Oh, sure.
We used to go to work and come home and, and, and get food from there.
If I was glad to have the work?
I don't know I was glad.
It was not exactly up to me.
But I, I was not happy to go to work, no.
It was better to have work, right.
It was better to be employed than not to be employed.
Well, this started when they didn't have no employment, if they were not [INAUDIBLE], as they used to call it.
They picked them up and they sent them out to-- supposed to be a transport sending out to the east.
You never heard from them again.
We worked from 19-- I worked from 1939, starting late in 1939, till, till 1941.
I worked in Nowy Targ, my hometown.
In 1941, I was sent to Zakopane, which is not too far from Nowy Targ, about seven kilometers.
It's a little city right on the-- on the border.
And that's, what they say, [SPEAKING POLISH].
Resort, yeah.
This was a winter and summer resort.

The minute-- the minute the Germans came in 1939, they requested all the Jews move out from Zakopane, because it was like a-- like special made for Germans to, to come for the winter, for the winter and the summer resort.
And Jews were not allowed to live in Zakopane.
Was Judenfrei.
And we were shipped to Zakopane.
And we was-- we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were put in the barracks.
Was a stone quarry.
And we had to work 12 hours a day in the stone quarry, from morning till night, not, not 12 hours, something.
In the wintertime, we [? didn't ?] work 12 hours.
And we used to [INAUDIBLE] the, the big-- dynamite the big mountains, chop it up with [INAUDIBLE].
And there used to be machines which they chopped up to a small, little, little gravel.
And there were-- there were little stones, bigger stones, and you know, [INAUDIBLE].
And, and we loaded things on the trucks and shipped it on the big railroad tracks to a different part of, I guess, of the country, to which they build roads.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was there since-- from 1941 till 1942.
Yes, I do.
The head of the Jewish council, Jewish Judenrat, was Ginsberg.
I forget the name-- Ginsberg.
First name, I don't remember.

Before the war he was-- he was-- in the Jewish community, he used to be a-- what they call it, a [? expediter. ?] In other words, he used to-- they used to have horses and bring merchandise from, from the railroad to the stores.
What would you call that?
It's, it's not like a moving company, like-- you know, what they call it?
What they call-- would you call it?
I'm not sure.
Trading?
I'm not sure.
No, no, no, no.
He-- today, you pick up merchandise from, from the-- from the railroads to the stores with big trucks, right?
And in Poland, they didn't have no trucks.
They used to-- they used to have horses.
And he used to deliver merchandise, you know, from the-- from the railroad or from the, you know, to the stores.
If somebody ships out merchandise-- it was like shipping.
And they were-- they, they, they were known people in, in the city, like, you know, like respectable people.
He was named Meyer Ginsberg, I remember.
Very much so.
Very much so.
You see, they tried-- well, it was bad.
They had to provide so many people to work.

But they tried to provide young people, young boys, which they didn't-- you know, not that the family should suffer, not to send out people, you know, a little bit older people, or people from the families.
Well, in the beginning, they did it-- as I said, they used to send only young people, young teenagers, or young people.
Later on, they were-- they were-- they were short of young people.
They had to send everybody they, they could get a hold of.
OK.
